tree.title: Vector Projection
tree.summary: This tutorial explains vector projection, covering its definition, mathematical formula, unit vector representation, practical applications, and provides interactive experiments to help visualize how one vector projects onto another.

children.title: 1. Interactive Playground
children.summary: This section describes an interactive tool where users can manipulate vector endpoints by dragging or editing values to visualize the projection of vector a onto vector b, which is displayed in green.

children.title: Interactive Vector Projection Playground
children.summary: An interactive tool where users can manipulate vector endpoints and see the projection of vector a onto vector b displayed in green.
children.sourceText: 1. Interactive Playground] Drag the endpoints of the vectors, or edit the values directly below. The  green vector  shows the projection of  a  onto  b .

sourceAnchor.startMarker: 1. Interactive Playground] Drag the endpoints of the vectors
sourceAnchor.endMarker: shows the projection of  a  onto  b .

children.title: 2. What is Vector Projection?
children.summary: Vector projection finds the "shadow" or component of one vector that lies along the direction of another vector, essentially determining how much of the first vector points in the same direction as the second.

children.title: Vector Projection Definition and Streetlight Analogy
children.summary: Vector projection is explained through a streetlight shadow analogy, showing how one vector creates a 'shadow' onto another vector.
children.sourceText: Imagine you're standing under a streetlight, and your shadow falls on the ground. The shadow is a projection of you onto the ground plane. Vector projection works the same way: we're finding the "shadow" of one vector onto another.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Imagine you're standing under a streetlight, and your shadow falls on the ground.
sourceAnchor.endMarker: we're finding the "shadow" of one vector onto another.

children.title: Mathematical Purpose of Vector Projection
children.summary: Vector projection finds the component of one vector that lies along the direction of another vector.
children.sourceText:
Specifically, we want to find the component of vector  a  that lies along the direction of vector  b . 
The projection answers: "How much of a points in the same direction as b?"

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Specifically, we want to find the component of vector  a  that lies along the direction of vector  b .
sourceAnchor.endMarker: How much of a points in the same direction as b?"

children.title: 3. The Formula
children.summary: The projection of vector a onto vector b is calculated using the formula proj_b(a) = ((a · b) / |b|²) × b, which measures how much of vector a points in the same direction as vector b.

children.title: Vector Projection Formula Introduction
children.summary: The basic formula for projecting vector a onto vector b is presented with its mathematical notation.
children.sourceText:
The projection of vector  a  onto vector  b  is: 
proj_b(a) = ((a · b) / |b|²) × b Let's break this down:

sourceAnchor.startMarker: The projection of vector  a  onto vector  b  is:
sourceAnchor.endMarker: Let's break this down:

children.title: When Vectors Are Equal: Pythagorean Connection
children.summary: Explores what happens when vector A equals vector B and how this relates to the Pythagorean theorem through dot products.
children.sourceText:
Hey, if vector A were the same as vector B, wouldn't this just be the Pythagorean formula? a*a + b*b = c * c?] 
[CALLOUT: About "a₁×b₁ + a₂×b₂" You're absolutely right to notice that connection! When vector A equals vector B, the dot product A·A does become a₁² + a₂², which is indeed related to the Pythagorean theorem. This gives us the square of the vector's length (or magnitude). 
However, there's a subtle but important difference from the traditional Pythagorean formula c² = a² + b². In the vector case, a₁ and a₂ are the components of a single vector - they're like the horizontal and vertical "legs" of that vector. So a₁² + a₂² equals the square of the vector's length, not the length itself. 
This is actually a key insight: the dot product of a vector with itself tells us how "long" that vector is (squared). But when we take the dot product of two different vectors, we're measuring something else entirely - how much they point in the same direction. The formula stays the same, but the geometric meaning changes completely depending on whether we're using the same vector twice or two different vectors.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Hey, if vector A were the same as vector B, wouldn't this just be the Pythagorean formula?
sourceAnchor.endMarker: The formula stays the same, but the geometric meaning changes completely depending on whether we're using the same vector twice or two different vectors.

children.title: Two Different Vectors and Right Triangles
children.summary: Demonstrates how dot products work with two different vectors using right triangles and explains the geometric relationship between vector projections.
children.sourceText:
If the vector is the hypotenuse, and the x and y components are the other sides, then show how this math works with two different vectors (ideally a diagram of two right triangles and the math of the dot product)] 
[CALLOUT: About "how much they point in the same direction" Picture two vectors: vector A pointing at (3,4) and vector B pointing at (6,2). Each forms its own right triangle with the axes - A has legs of 3 and 4, while B has legs of 6 and 2. 
When we compute their dot product A·B = (3)(6) + (4)(2) = 18 + 8 = 26, we're not just multiplying corresponding components randomly. We're actually measuring how much of vector A's "effort" goes in the same direction as vector B. Think of it this way: if you project A onto B's direction, the dot product tells you the length of that projection multiplied by B's length. 
The key insight is that while each vector individually uses the Pythagorean theorem (A's length is √(3²+4²) = 5, B's length is √(6²+2²) = √40), the dot product captures something different entirely. It's measuring the geometric relationship between the two triangles - specifically, how aligned their hypotenuses are. If the vectors pointed in exactly the same direction, the dot product would equal the product of their lengths. If they were perpendicular, it would be zero, regardless of how long each hypotenuse is.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: If the vector is the hypotenuse, and the x and y components are the other sides, then show how this math works with two different vectors
sourceAnchor.endMarker: If they were perpendicular, it would be zero, regardless of how long each hypotenuse is.

children.title: Why Multiplication Instead of Addition
children.summary: Explains why dot products use multiplication to capture directional agreement between vectors rather than addition which would miss alignment information.
children.sourceText:
Why do we use multiplication here instead of addition?] 
[CALLOUT: About "dot product" Multiplication captures how much two vectors "agree" in each direction, while addition would just combine their magnitudes without considering alignment. 
Think of it this way: if both vectors point strongly in the same direction (both components are positive and large), multiplying gives you a big positive number, showing strong agreement. If they point in opposite directions (one positive, one negative), multiplication gives you a negative number, showing disagreement. Addition would miss this directional relationship entirely. 
For example, if vector a = (3, 4) and vector b = (2, 1), the dot product is 3×2 + 4×1 = 10. Both vectors have positive components, so they generally point in the same direction. But if b = (-2, -1), you get 3×(-2) + 4×(-1) = -10, showing they point in opposite directions. If we used addition instead (3+2 + 4+1 = 10 vs 3+(-2) + 4+(-1) = 4), we'd lose this crucial directional information that tells us whether the vectors align or oppose each other. 
This directional agreement is exactly what we need for vector projection - we need to know how much one vector extends in the direction of another.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Why do we use multiplication here instead of addition?
sourceAnchor.endMarker: we need to know how much one vector extends in the direction of another.

children.title: Fourier Transform Connection and Resonance Pattern
children.summary: Draws parallels between dot products and Fourier transforms, showing how both measure agreement or resonance between mathematical objects.
children.sourceText:
Hey, this is like (naive) Fourier transforms! You look at each point of a signal and multiply it against the sin value of a particular frequency, and if there's a wave at that frequency in your signal, sin(wave) * signal(thatpoint) will always be positive! Great observation! You've hit on a fundamental pattern that appears throughout mathematics and signal processing. In Fourier transforms, you're essentially measuring how much your signal "agrees" with a specific frequency by multiplying them pointwise - when they're in phase (both positive or both negative), you get positive contributions that accumulate, and when they're out of phase, you get cancellation. 
This same principle is exactly what's happening in vector dot products. When we multiply corresponding components of two vectors, we're checking their agreement in each dimensional direction. If both vectors point in the same direction along an axis, we get positive contribution. If they point in opposite directions, we get negative contribution (disagreement). The final dot product sums up all these directional agreements, giving us a single number that captures the overall alignment between the vectors. 
This is why the dot product is so central to vector projection - it's literally measuring how much one vector "resonates" with another, just like your Fourier example measures how much a signal resonates with a particular frequency. That's also why we can use the dot product to find the component of one vector that lies along another vector's direction, which is the essence of projection.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Hey, this is like (naive) Fourier transforms! You look at each point of a signal
sourceAnchor.endMarker: which is the essence of projection.

children.title: Converting to Scalar Value
children.summary: Explains how the dot product division creates a scalar that measures how much of one vector lies in another's direction.
children.sourceText: When we divide the dot product by the squared length of vector b, we're converting a vector operation into a simple number. This scalar is crucial because it gives us the exact measurement of how much of vector a lies in the direction of vector b, expressed as a multiple of b's length.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: When we divide the dot product by the squared length of vector b, we're converting a vector operation into a simple number.
sourceAnchor.endMarker: expressed as a multiple of b's length.

children.title: 4. Alternative Form: Unit Vectors
children.summary: When vector b is normalized to a unit vector b̂, the projection formula simplifies to proj_b(a) = (a · b̂) × b̂, where the dot product directly gives the scalar projection length.

children.title: Normalizing Vector b to Unit Vector
children.summary: The first step involves converting vector b into a unit vector b̂ with length 1.
children.sourceText: If we first normalize  b  to a unit vector  b̂  (length = 1), the formula simplifies:

sourceAnchor.startMarker: If we first normalize  b  to a unit vector
sourceAnchor.endMarker: the formula simplifies:

children.title: Simplified Projection Formula with Unit Vectors
children.summary: When using unit vectors, the projection formula becomes proj_b(a) = (a · b̂) × b̂.
children.sourceText: proj_b(a) = (a · b̂) × b̂ Here, (a · b̂) directly gives us the scalar projection — the signed length of the projection.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: proj_b(a) = (a · b̂) × b̂
sourceAnchor.endMarker: the signed length of the projection.

children.title: Converting Scalar to Vector Projection
children.summary: Multiplying the scalar projection by the unit vector b̂ converts it back to a vector form.
children.sourceText: Multiplying by b̂ converts it back to a vector.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Multiplying by b̂ converts it back to a vector.
sourceAnchor.endMarker: Multiplying by b̂ converts it back to a vector.

children.title: 5. Why Does This Matter?
children.summary: Vector projection is widely used across various technical fields due to its fundamental importance in mathematical applications.

children.title: Importance of Vector Projection in Technical Fields
children.summary: Vector projection is a fundamental concept that appears extensively across various technical disciplines.
children.sourceText: Vector projection appears everywhere in technical fields:

children.title: 6. Experiments to Try
children.summary: This section encourages readers to return to a previous visualization and conduct experiments, with a hint that projection magnitude depends only on the angle between vectors, not the length of vector b.

children.title: Experiments Introduction and Setup
children.summary: Introduction to hands-on experiments using the visualization above to explore vector projection concepts.
children.sourceText: Go back to the visualization above and try these experiments:

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Go back to the visualization above and try these experiments:
sourceAnchor.endMarker: Go back to the visualization above and try these experiments:

children.title: Key Insight About Projection Magnitude
children.summary: The projection's magnitude depends only on the angle between vectors, not on the length of vector b.
children.sourceText: Hint: The projection's magnitude only depends on the angle between the vectors, not on the length of b!

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Hint: The projection's magnitude only depends on the angle between the vectors, not on the length of b!
sourceAnchor.endMarker: Hint: The projection's magnitude only depends on the angle between the vectors, not on the length of b!

children.title: Section 7
children.summary: The scalar in vector projection, calculated as (a·b)/|b|², determines how far along vector b the projection extends, serving as a coordinate that specifies the exact location of the projected point on b's direction.

children.title: The Scalar as Heart of Vector Projection
children.summary: The scalar from vector projection tells us exactly how far along vector b the projection extends, providing the crucial coordinate information.
children.sourceText: This scalar is the heart of vector projection - it's the coordinate that tells us exactly where the projection lands on vector b. Without it, we'd just know the direction (which is b's direction), but not how far to travel along that direction to reach our projected point

sourceAnchor.startMarker: This scalar is the heart of vector projection
sourceAnchor.endMarker: to reach our projected point

children.title: Walking Along a Path Example Calculation
children.summary: A concrete example showing how to calculate the projection scalar using vectors a and b, resulting in traveling 0.6 times the length of b.
children.sourceText:
Say vector b = (3, 4) has length 5. After computing the dot product a·b = 15, we divide by |b|² = 25 to get scalar 0.6. 
scalar = (a·b) / |b|² = 15/25 = 0.6 This means: travel 0.6 times the full length of b to reach the projection. Since b has length 5, we travel 0.6 × 5 = 3 units along b's direction

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Say vector b = (3, 4) has length 5
sourceAnchor.endMarker: we travel 0.6 × 5 = 3 units along b's direction

children.title: GPS Highway Analogy for Understanding Scalars
children.summary: The scalar acts like GPS coordinates on a highway, telling you exactly how far along the highway to go as a fraction of reference distance.
children.sourceText: Think of it like GPS coordinates on a highway. Vector b is the highway, and this scalar is like saying 'your destination is at mile marker 0.6' - it tells you exactly how far along the highway to go, as a fraction of some reference distance

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Think of it like GPS coordinates on a highway
sourceAnchor.endMarker: as a fraction of some reference distance

children.title: Complete Projection Vector Construction
children.summary: To get the actual projection vector, multiply the scalar by the unit vector of b, combining 'how much' with 'which way' information.
children.sourceText: To find the actual projection vector, multiply this scalar by the unit vector of b. The scalar tells you 'how much', and b's direction tells you 'which way' - together they give you the complete projection

sourceAnchor.startMarker: To find the actual projection vector, multiply this scalar
sourceAnchor.endMarker: together they give you the complete projection

children.title: Section 8
children.summary: This section explains how vector projection can be extended from projecting onto a line (1D) to projecting onto a plane (2D), where you project a 3D vector onto a plane defined by two basis vectors by finding how much the vector aligns with each plane direction and combining those components.

children.title: Projecting 3D Vectors Onto Planes Concept
children.summary: Introduction to projecting 3D vectors onto planes as a natural extension from projecting onto lines.
children.sourceText:
Yes, this would make an excellent extension! Projecting a 3D vector onto a plane is the natural next step from what you've learned about projecting onto lines. 
Here's how it connects: Instead of projecting onto a single vector (which gives you a point on a line), you're projecting onto a plane defined by two vectors.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Yes, this would make an excellent extension! Projecting a 3D vector onto a plane
sourceAnchor.endMarker: you're projecting onto a plane defined by two vectors.

children.title: Shadow Analogy for Plane Projection
children.summary: Visual analogy comparing plane projection to dropping a shadow onto tilted paper in 3D space.
children.sourceText: Think of it like dropping a shadow onto a tilted piece of paper in 3D space. The plane is spanned by your two basis vectors, and your projection will be some combination of those two vectors that gets you as close as possible to your original vector while staying in the plane.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: Think of it like dropping a shadow onto a tilted piece of paper in 3D space.
sourceAnchor.endMarker: as close as possible to your original vector while staying in the plane.

children.title: Mathematical Process for Plane Projection
children.summary: Explanation of how to calculate projections using dot products with two basis vectors instead of one.
children.sourceText: The process mirrors what you've seen: you'd find how much of your vector aligns with each of the two plane vectors (using dot products), then combine those components. It's like the 2D case but extended - instead of one component along one direction, you get two components along two directions that define your plane.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: The process mirrors what you've seen: you'd find how much of your vector aligns
sourceAnchor.endMarker: you get two components along two directions that define your plane.

children.title: Scaling from 1D to 2D Projections
children.summary: How projection concepts scale from projecting onto lines to projecting onto planes with visualization benefits.
children.sourceText: This extension would beautifully demonstrate how projection scales from 1D (onto a line) to 2D (onto a plane), and you could visualize how changing the plane's orientation affects where the shadow falls. The same geometric intuition about "closest point" and "perpendicular distance" applies, just in higher dimensions.

sourceAnchor.startMarker: This extension would beautifully demonstrate how projection scales from 1D (onto a line) to 2D
sourceAnchor.endMarker: The same geometric intuition about "closest point" and "perpendicular distance" applies, just in higher dimensions.
